Rob Carlson

Education: PhD in Physics, Princeton University, 1997. BS in Physics with College Honors, University of Washington, 1993.

Current Position: Research Fellow at the The Molecular Sciences Insitute, Berkeley, CA.

I am interested in technology of all kinds, but I spend most of my time on biological technology. By this I mean two things, 1) I work on methods to model and measure biology in new ways, and 2) I want to build things using biological components.

On the academic side: Building new gadgets to measure biology in new ways.

Testing any predictive model of biology will require more detailed measurements than are now possible. To that end, what I really want is a Tricorder, á la Star Trek. There isn't any physical reason we can't have a gizmo that measures all the molecules in a functioning cell, it's just that we don't yet know how to build it. To be sure, it is not clear that all of this information would be useful in understanding biology, but we certainly require better tools than exist now. I am working on methods of identifying and quantifying proteins from cellular extracts with the aim of creating assays for use with single cells.

Biology as Technology:

Biology will eventually become a human technology in itself, and I have been thinking and writing about how this process may play out.

"Biological Technology in 2050" received a Silver Award in The World In 2050 Essay Competition run by Shell and The Economist. This essay explores possible consequence of distributed biologal technology for the structure of labor and the economy as a whole. This essay has recently been translated into Russian.

"The Pace of Biological Technologies" is currently in draft form. I compare the rates of improvement in DNA sequencing and synthesis with Moore's law, comment on the similarities and differences between IC manufacturing technology and biological technology, and briefly consider economic implications.

I want to create measurement techniques that help me to explore evolution and biological computation, specifically the computational capacity of single cells. How smart are single cells? What kinds of problems can they solve? How do they do it? Improving human health or building gizmos using biological components will require understanding and reprogramming single cells, the fundamental unit of biology.

Cancer, for example, is predominantly a disease of a single cell. Generally it is not a whole organ or tissue that becomes cancerous, but rather one cell that breaks loose from its developmental pathway -- due to chance mutations, changes in the environment, or infection -- and runs amok.

Asking these sorts of questions requires tools that are on the same scale as the cells themselves. In previous work, I used microfabrication to build fluidic systems with length scales of microns to tens of microns to investigate the effects of physiological deformation on human leukocytes. This work is an example of a new kind of tool built on a micro scale which elucidates new biology. However, most microfabrication efforts are aimed at miniturizing current laboratory apparatus. This approach may provide information faster and at less cost, but the important point is that it provides the same sort of information as is available now. Instead of working on this miniturization effort, I am focusing on building gadgets and gizmos that provide new kinds of information.

Photo: Glissading down Mt. Hood, Oregon, 1994.
